items.title: SNAP FY2026 benefit amounts and income limits are in effect through Sept. 30, 2026
items.summary: USDA updated SNAP FY2026 standards effective Oct. 1, 2025 through Sept. 30, 2026, including a maximum monthly allotment of $994 for a 4-person household in the 48 states/DC (and $298 for a 1-person household). The minimum monthly allotment for 1–2 person households in the 48 states/DC is $24, and the gross monthly income limit for a 4-person household is $3,483 (130% of the federal poverty level).

items.title: USDA approved six more state SNAP waivers to remove certain foods from eligibility starting in 2026
items.summary: USDA announced it approved SNAP waivers submitted by Hawai‘i, Missouri, North Dakota, South Carolina, Virginia, and Tennessee that will amend the statutory definition of “food for purchase” under SNAP beginning in 2026. USDA said the waivers are intended to remove certain “unhealthy” foods (described as highly processed/junk foods) to promote healthier choices.

items.title: CMS guidance outlines Oct. 1 limits on federal Medicaid/CHIP matching funds for certain noncitizens
items.summary: CMS issued guidance on April 8, 2026 to implement a reconciliation-law provision affecting federal matching funds for full Medicaid and CHIP benefits. Beginning Oct. 1, federal match for full benefits will be restricted to legal permanent residents, certain Cuban and Haitian immigrants, and Compact of Free Association migrants lawfully residing in the U.S., while federal match remains available for emergency Medicaid.

items.title: SSI federal payment amounts increased for 2026 (effective January 2026)
items.summary: SSA reports that the 2026 cost-of-living increase for SSI is 2.8%, effective January 2026. The monthly maximum federal SSI amounts for 2026 are $994 for an eligible individual, $1,491 for an eligible individual with an eligible spouse, and $498 for an essential person.

items.title: HUD ends EHV/Stability Voucher waiver that allowed delayed SSN/citizenship documentation
items.summary: HUD’s PIH Notice 2026-05 removes (strikes) the waiver/alternative requirement that previously allowed some Emergency Housing Voucher and Stability Voucher applicants to be admitted without SSN or eligible noncitizen documentation, as long as documents were provided within 180 days. HUD says PHAs must follow the standard SSN verification rules (24 CFR 5.210–5.218) and citizenship/immigration documentation requirements (24 CFR 5.508).
items.source: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development

items.title: WIC FY2026 fruit & vegetable cash-value benefit amounts set; states must allow at least one non-fresh option by April 2026
items.summary: USDA WIC Policy Memorandum #2026-2 sets FY2026 monthly cash-value voucher/benefit (CVV/B) amounts at $26 for children, $48 for pregnant/postpartum participants, and $52 for fully/mostly breastfeeding participants, reflecting an 11.8% inflation adjustment over FY2022 baseline amounts. The memo also notes WIC food package changes require state agencies to authorize at least one form of fruits and vegetables beyond fresh (frozen, canned, and/or dried) by April 2026.

items.title: Federal Student Aid says the maximum Pell Grant for award year 2026–27 is $7,395
items.summary: Federal Student Aid’s consumer guidance states that for the 2026–27 award year, the maximum Federal Pell Grant award is $7,395. The article encourages students to submit the FAFSA to be considered for Pell and other aid.

items.title: ED guidance: Pell Grant ineligibility when Student Aid Index is at least twice the maximum Pell (2026–27)
items.summary: The FSA Handbook guidance for 2026–27 explains a new Pell eligibility limit: students are ineligible for Pell if their Student Aid Index (SAI) equals or exceeds twice the maximum Pell Grant amount (example given: if max Pell is $7,395, the cutoff SAI is 14,790). The same guidance also notes that beginning with 2026–27, the foreign earned income exclusion is added to AGI for determining automatic maximum/minimum Pell eligibility.

items.title: IRS: 2026 EITC maximum increases to $8,231 for families with 3+ qualifying children
items.summary: The IRS announced annual inflation adjustments for tax year 2026, including that the maximum Earned Income Tax Credit is $8,231 for taxpayers with three or more qualifying children (up from $8,046 for 2025). IRS also notes the investment income limit for claiming EITC for taxable years beginning in 2026 is $12,200.

items.title: IRS: Child Tax Credit for tax year 2026 is $2,200 per qualifying child; refundable portion amount is $1,700
items.summary: IRS Revenue Procedure 2025-32 lists the inflation-adjusted child tax credit amounts for taxable years beginning in 2026: the maximum credit under IRC §24(a) is $2,200, and the amount used to determine the refundable portion under §24(d)(1)(A) is $1,700. These figures apply to tax year 2026 (returns typically filed in 2027).
